rationale: The citing paper uses Chevalier and Mayzlin (2006) as core prior evidence that online WOM valence affects sales and that negative WOM can be more influential than positive WOM. It also adopts concrete design choices from that study, explicitly following its WOM valence measures (average rating and one-star/five-star shares) and its lag structure linking earlier review changes to later sales-rank changes. Beyond background listing, this citation helps shape both hypothesis framing and empirical setup in the citing paper. These roles indicate a meaningful, not merely passing, citation function.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Chevalier and Mayzlin's WOM-sales framework and empirical measurement approach, then adds observational learning and a natural-experiment design to separate and compare WOM versus OL effects. In doing so, it extends the prior online WOM-sales line to a broader social-influence setting with interaction and lifecycle analyses.
